data.title: Gerdau S.A. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Gerdau S.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Jobs Revisions Shock - July's Report Also Disappoints
data.description:
NEW YORK (August 1) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) stunned the markets with a disappointing 73,000 new jobs for July, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey.

The number was well below the consensus estimate of 106,000. The private payroll number was 83,000, after taking account of 10,000 government jobs lost.

Today’s report aligned with last month’s private payroll figure,

Notably, government revisions for May showed 50,000 fewer jobs and only 11,000 new jobs in June for an average of 19,500 jobs lost. After showing 73,000 new jobs in June. We note this paragraph from the June jobs report:

Employment in state government increased by 47,000, largely in education (+40,000). Employment in local government education continued to trend up (+23,000). Job losses continued in the federal government (-7,000), where employment is down by 69,000 since reaching a recent peak in January. (Employees on paid leave or receiving ongoing severance pay are counted as employed in the establishment survey.) [Emphasis is mine.]

We suspect that the Federal DOGE layoffs took effect in July after employees completed their severance period. But what shocked this analyst (and the markets) was that May and June job creation figures were revised down by 258,000 jobs. The average new non-farm job creation figure for the last three months is just 48,500 new jobs.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 200,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate both ticked up to 4.2% and 7.9%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 38,000 people left the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate down slightly, to 62.2% from 62.3% in June.

Today’s numbers made yesterday’s ADP jobs report, which showed 104,000 jobs lost, seemingly inapposite for observers of the labor economy. But June’s ADP that showed 33,000 new jobs now seems appropriate, given today’s June revisions.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of June and July Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by Financial Services, as accounting, banking, and other financial firms hire on new staff and recent graduates to train them for their roles. The other gainer was in lower-wage jobs in hospitality. Retail gained 15,700 jobs after losing 14,300 jobs in June, so it was a veritable "wash". Lower-wage hospitality gained 5,000 new jobs. Manufacturing lost 11,000 jobs, all in the non-durable manufacturing sector, which, of note, includes food processors that have reputedly had a large number of illegal alien laborers. Durable goods manufacturing remained at the status quo.

We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but the government lost 10,000 jobs, as discussed above.

Analysis

Economy Generally

Today’s numbers resuscitate our recession concerns that we had back in January. We had all but put them to rest based on the jobs reports that now seem to have been gravely misleading.

As we explained in the June jobs report:

As we explained back in January, historically, a recession is almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve is resolved. We’re still not yet ready to dismiss that thinking at the start of the third quarter, but we are reducing our level of confidence and judging it to be less likely than more likely.

But we added,

That said, the data of late does show the economy is slowing somewhat. First quarter GDP printed basically down at -0.5% in the third and final estimate. So-called “Real GDP” - GDP minus elements of “noise”, and formally referred to as “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 1.9% in the revised estimate.

That “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers” printed at just 1.2% in the preliminary estimate of Q2'25, further indicates a slower economy.

Inflation

We have thought for some time that the threat to inflation in the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs, as we explained in this article from last November.

JP Morgan CEO Jamie Dimon, who had been critical of Trump’s tariffs, has come to align with that view. He reversed his view of tariffs as of yesterday, saying, “They may drive slightly higher inflation, but what you really want is more growth.” But Dimon's comments were made before this morning’s jobs report, although, as best as we can determine, after yesterday’s GDP report.

The greater risk to inflation, in our view, is the decline in the USD Index (DXY), which printed today at just 99.

Nevertheless, we continue to believe the ongoing collapse of China’s economy, which we discussed here, will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT), which is known to aggressively push suppliers to lower wholesale prices, and we expect a number of other large U.S. importers will use the buyers' market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

The public also thinks inflation expectations seem to be overblown, with the 5-year inflation expectation basically at or around 2.32, where it was when Trump was sworn in, but up considerably from where it was in April, when tariff policy was first announced. It does seem to be declining after recent tariff agreements were announced with South Korea, the EU, and others.

Speaking of which, Europe seems to have changed its view with respect to the United States and President Trump, at least at the current impression. The flawless execution of Operation Midnight Hammer, the clandestine bombing of Iran’s nuclear sites. Europe now seems far more willing to accommodate and deal with the Trump governing agenda, as disruptive to the status quo as it may be. That said, France and, to a lesser extent, Germany seem to still oppose the Trump presidency.

Prognostications

The data point to a slowing economy. However, as we said in our 2025Q2 GDP report earlier this week, we think there may be substantially increased capex owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. However, that won’t be reflected, likely, until the later part of September and will not be reflected in 2025Q3 GDP. In view of today’s release, we are reducing our estimate of 2025 Q3 to just 1%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the Fed to lower rates by a quarter point in its September meeting.

Readers wishing a deeper dive on some of the other data points that affect our judgements are welcome to assess "Other Data Points" below.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 3% in Q2'25, according to the advance estimate released July 30th. Core GDP, or final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 1.2%, down from 1.9% in the 2025Q1. As we wrote in our GDP analysis earlier this week, there was a lot of “noise” in the GDP print owing to imports and inventories.

Core PCE Inflation, ex-food and fuel, reportedly the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation, was released July 31 and printed at a 2.8% annual rate for the month of June. PCE inflation printed at 2.6% Those figures are up from 2.7% and 2.3%, respectively, from May.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturers' Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) printed at 48, down a full point from 49.0 in June. The overall manufacturing sector has been declining faster for the last five months.

The June services Index grew to 50.8 from May’s 49.9. It will be a few days until the services index for July is released.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for May was released on Tuesday, with 275,000 fewer job openings than in May and 261,000 fewer hires.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in June were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,397,000. This is 0.2% above the revised May rate of 1,394,000 but is 4.4% below the June 2024 rate of 1,461,000.

Personal income increased $71.4 billion (0.3% at a monthly rate) in June, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $61.0 billion (0.3%), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $69.9 billion (0.3%).

The PCE price index for June increased 2.6% from the same month one year ago. Excluding food and energy, the PCE price index increased 2.8% from one year ago. From the preceding month, the PCE price index for June increased 0.3%. Excluding food and energy, the PCE price index also increased 0.3%.

Personal income increased $71.4 billion (0.3% at a monthly rate) in June, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $61.0 billion (0.3%), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $69.9 billion (0.3%).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for August has not been printed as of this writing. In July, it edged down from 49.2 in June to 48.6—a 1.2% decline.

_________________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe are underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies that we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Deserving Dip
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (NASDAQ:PANW) just shocked the markets with a major acquisition at a huge premium. The cybersecurity leader even suggests more deals are in the works to complete their platform goals. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock due to the subpar growth in comparison to the premium valuation.

CyberArk Deal

On January 30, Palo Alto Networks agreed to acquire CyberArk (CYBR) in a deal valued at $25 billion. The company surprised the market with the size of the acquisition and the minimal revenue acquired in the process.

The cybersecurity giant is paying $45 in cash plus 2.2005 shares of stock for each CyberArk share. The mostly stock deal definitely acknowledges Palo Alto is using premium equity to pay for a stock trading at a premium valuation.

CyberArk is a leader in Identity Security with a focus on Privileged Access Management (PAM). The deal suggests an expectation for AI to vastly boost the need for security access to systems due to the use of machines for Agentic AI, where AI agents dramatically increase the amount of identities to manage and distinguish between the security requirements of humans and AI agents. CyberArk reported more than 80x the machine identities to manage compared to humans.

The market forecast is for the Identity Security TAM to jump from $29 billion in 2025 to $47 billion by 2028 due to AI. Palo Alto Networks apparently had a gap in identity management, and CyberArk solved this issue, allowing the combined company to now target a combined $257 billion TAM in a few years.

While all of this sounds great, CyberArk was only targeting 2025 revenues of $1.3 billion. Along with the merger announcement, the company reported Q2 2025 sales grew by 46% to reach $328 million via a nearly $12 million beat.

Palo Alto Networks paid an incredible 19x sales targets. For this reason, the deal won't be free cash flow accretive until FY 2028.

The cybersecurity giant had a market cap of $136 billion when the deal was announced, so the dilution is somewhat minimal. Palo Alto Networks has 707 million diluted shares outstanding, and the deal will issue about 103 million shares to CyberArk shareholders.

With Palo Alto Networks collapsing to $170, the deal has dipped to only $419. CyberArk shareholders were promised a deal premium of 26% over the pre-offer announcement price of $380, and the stock is only up about 10% now.

Platformization Push

Palo Alto Networks continues to make a huge platformization push that hasn't led to improving financial results. The company went from 25% growth in FY 2023 prior to announcing the multi-platform growth to only 16% in the last reported quarter, with a target of 14% in the just ended FQ4.

The CyberArk deal and lack of product coverage in AI Identity Security reinforce the concern over a platform focus versus best of breed products. Palo Alto Networks has leading products in endpoints and other areas of cybersecurity, but the company lacks the full solutions, and new areas of importance always pop up with AI, making identity security even more important.

The stock has slipped over $30 from the recent highs, but Palo Alto Networks is still a $115 billion market cap. The standalone business targets FY 2025 revenues of $9.2 billion, reaching $10.5 billion in FY 2026.

Palo Alto trades at 11x FY 2026 revenue targets. Once adding the CyberArk dilution and the $1.6 billion in 2026 revenues, the combined company has a market value of $138 billion with a sales target in the $12 billion range, leaving the stock trading at an expensive 14x sales forecast.

The deal requires a cash payment of $45 per CyberArk share, leading to a payout of $2.3 billion. Palo Alto Networks already has a cash balance of $8.5 billion with annual free cash flow generation of over $3 billion, again suggesting the stock premiums were limiting the interest in cashing out at these levels despite an abundance of cash.

The company is likely to continue making additional acquisitions to complete the platform process after buying Protect AI earlier this year and other firms in prior years. Apparently, Palo Alto Networks can't build the systems internally with the reality of cybersecurity always having a hard time creating one system or platform to cover all security needs constantly pushing customers to stronger point products.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Palo Alto Networks remains an expensive stock without the required growth rates. Even after the stock has fallen over $30 from announcing the deal to acquire CyberArk, the stock is still expensive.

Investors should use any rallied to sell the stock.

data.title: Ares Management Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Ares Management Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Amicus Therapeutics: Going Beyond $1B Target Revenues Of Lead Products With Licensed DMX-200
data.description:
Amicus Therapeutics (NASDAQ:FOLD) is a very interesting biotech because it is continuing to build momentum with respect to the commercialization of both of its approved drug candidates, POMBILITI and OPFOLDA, for the treatment of patients with late-stage Pompe Disease [PD] and GALAFOLD for the treatment of adults with Fabry Disease [FD]. As a matter of fact, the company expects revenues from all of these candidates to allow it to generate $1 billion in revenue by 2028.

More importantly, each of these candidates alone is expected to exceed $1 billion or more in revenues. This story of Amicus alone makes it exciting, but it made an even better move, one that could end up helping it achieve a higher bottom line of its earnings. Of course that's if it eventually receives approval for it. What I'm talking about is a clinical candidate licensed from Dimerix for DMX-200 for the treatment of patients with Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis [FSGS] kidney disease. The use of this small-molecule inhibitor of chemokine receptor 2 [CCR2] is being explored in the phase 3 ACTION3 study. The importance of this program is that it is to bring about several catalysts. The first catalyst of which would be the completed enrollment of this phase 3 study by the end of 2025.

The downside is that the study itself will not be completed until 2029. If this is the case, why bring it up now? The reason why is because there will be two interim analysis time points done a lot earlier than the completion of the study itself. There was a Type C meeting in March of 2025 with the FDA whereby proteinuria would be classified as an appropriate primary endpoint for approval of DMX-200 for the treatment of patients with FSGS. The last point I want to make is that the move to license DMX-200 was not smart only on this one indication in the United States alone, but that the license itself covers all other possible indications that this candidate could be developed for.

DMX-200 Licensing Brings About Huge Market Opportunity To Boost Shareholder Value

As I noted above, Amicus Therapeutics announced an agreement with Dimerix whereby it would license its small molecule inhibitor of the chemokine receptor 2 [CCR2] DMX-200 for the treatment of patients with Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. The use of this candidate for the treatment of these patients with this rare kidney disorder is being explored in the ongoing phase 3 ACTION3 study. Before going over this study in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, it is first important to go over what it is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. Focal segmental Glomerulosclerosis [FSGS] is a type of rare kidney disorder where some sections of the kidney filtering units have scar tissue. This scar tissue is what leads to reduced kidney function for these patients. As with all other types of kidney disorders, the inability to filter out waste properly leads to increased protein and blood in urine.

This leads me to a very important point on why Amicus and Dimerix have a good drug with DMX-200, which is that there are no approved drugs for it. The only option that patients would have to help treat their disease would be a kidney transplant or receiving dialysis. If not successfully treated, then it becomes very dire for the patient. How so? Well, if not treated effectively, it can lead to kidney failure. There are 4 types of FSGS, but the two main types are primary [idiopathic] FSGS, which occurs with no known cause, and secondary FSGS, which can be triggered from several causes, such as the following:

  • Other disorders like HIV and more
  • Infections
  • Toxicity from certain drugs being taken

The global focal segmental glomerulosclerosis market is expected to reach $51.72 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity, and there is one other very important fact to cite from this very same link, which is that the North American market had the largest revenue share of it with 47.4% in 2024. How can this drug actually help patients? Current therapy being given, known as Angiotensin II Receptor Blocker [ARB] lower blood pressure in hopes of causing less kidney strain. However, DMX-200 is put into place to target inflammation that occurs as a result of FSGS. The purpose of lowering inflammation is because when it is fully in place, it causes cells of the kidney to die, leading to more leakiness occurring. In turn, this leaking results in more protein being spilled into urine. Also, reduction of inflammation results in less scarring as well.

In order to see if DMX-200 is going to be able to help treat these patients with FSGS, it is in the process of exploring it in the ongoing phase 3 ACTION3 study. This study is expected to recruit up to a total of 286 patients who are going to be randomized to receive one of the following doses as follows for 104 weeks:

  • 120 mg immediate release capsules of DMX-200 twice daily [BID] dosing
  • 120 mg immediate release capsules of placebo BID dosing

It is quite important to note that this trial is not going to be using DMX-200 alone to treat these patients. When patients are given either therapy or placebo, they are also to be on concurrent background ARB. ARB is what I talked about above, which is used to lower blood pressure of the kidneys. This program is coming along well so far, and the hope is that it continues to see a successful outcome. The reason why I say to "continue" to see a successful outcome is because back in March of 2024 it was noted that the company was able to meet on the first pre-specified interim analysis of proteinuria. That is, it was shown that DMX-200 was performing better than placebo. There are two important things to state right away. First, this was the first interim analysis time point. A second blinded interim analysis is expected to take place once the primary and secondary endpoints have been pre-specified to the FDA. This goes back to the notion that in March of 2025, a Type C meeting with the FDA established proteinuria as an approvable endpoint for this phase 3 ACTION3 study. The second item to note is that this shows a positive trend of this proteinuria measure, but there is no guarantee that the second blinded interim analysis data will achieve the same outcome. Nor that the final data is going to achieve the primary endpoint of this late-stage trial in a statistically significant manner.

It remains to be seen if the second blinded interim analysis is positive, which would be huge news for both Dimerix and Amicus. However, this first positive interim analysis was ideal. Along with the fact that a prior phase 2 study [albeit only 8 patients] showed that the drug was able to reduce proteinuria with an average of 17% after 16 weeks versus placebo. The bad news is that Amicus had to give up some cash now and then more cash in the future to license DMX-200 for the treatment of these patients with FSGS. It had to give Dimerix an upfront payment of $30 million and then would be responsible for handing over an additional $560 million for successful U.S.-based milestone payments. Along with the tiered royalties of net sales on a successful product. Plus, Amicus would be responsible for funding the dossier to be given to the FDA and also the commercialization as well. That's a lot of stuff it has to pay out; are there any positives? Yes, actually there are two. The first of which is that Dimerix is responsible for the cost of completing the phase 3 study for starters. Secondly, under this exclusive license deal, Amicus is to be able to use DMX-200 as an oral small molecule inhibitor of CCR2 for any other indication it wants to go after. The point here is that the targeting of FSGS with this drug is only the beginning, as there is potential to use this drug far beyond this only. For Amicus specifically, it is important to note that this drug for the treatment of these rare kidney disorder patients was granted Orphan Drug Designation already [ODD].

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Amicus Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $231 million as of June 30, 2025. The reason for the decrease in cash is because of the $30 million upfront payment it had to give Dimerix to license DMX-200 for FSGS and other indications in the United States. The truth is that this company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand. This is not solely based on my estimate, but instead on what it had noted in its 10-Q SEC Filing, which is that it believes it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. It has been able to raise cash to date by stock offerings, debt issuance, collaboration agreements, and other financial tools.

A good thing is that should it need to raise additional cash, it could do so with a Sales Agreement it made with Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, whereby it could offer and sell up to an aggregate of $250 million. The good news with respect to this ATM is that it still has plenty of shares it can sell from it to generate cash. As a matter of fact, as of June 30, 2025, it has approximately $164.2 million shares left that could be issued and sold. The final point about the financials is that this company is generating revenues from its approved products. Both of its approved therapies achieved double-digit percentage sales growth in Q2 of 2025. GALAFOLD, for instance, reached $128.9 million [up 12% at Constant exchange rate [CER]]. Then, POMBILITI + OPFOLDA saw $25.8 million [up 58% CER]. In essence, the company notes that it will achieve $1 billion in sales for both products by 2028.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Amicus Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 3 ACTION3 study, which is using DMX-200 to treat patients with FSGS. A second blinded interim analysis is planned to happen for this late-stage trial, and there is no assurance that upon the release of this update that the drug will continue to outpace placebo in terms of proteinuria or eGFR slope over a certain period of time. A failure in the next interim analysis could spell doom for Amicus and Dimerix for this DMX-200 program targeting FSGS going forward.

A second risk to consider could then be a regulatory application filing of DMX-200 for the treatment of patients with FSGS. The reason why is because even if the company can file a regulatory application for approval of this oral small molecule inhibitor of CCR2 through the use of a surrogate endpoint, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to grant U.S. marketing approval of it. In addition, another risk to consider would be if this drug ends up being sold on the market. Even with FDA approval given, there is no guarantee that Amicus will be able to generate sufficient revenues from it.

A third risk to consider would be for any other expansion opportunity indication that Amicus Therapeutics chooses to target. As I have noted above, this drug candidate can be developed to go beyond that of targeting FSGS. That is, it is quite possible that it could be used to develop other rare renal indications. The risk is that even though the license agreement covers the ability to use DMX-200 to go after such other renal disorders in the United States, there is no guarantee that these specific trials are going to achieve statistical significance based on their respective primary endpoints.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be that of the competitors in place. For instance, there are several other competitors who are working on developing a drug to treat FSGS. For instance, Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) is developing its drug INAXAPLIN [VX-147] for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD]. However, a good thing here is that this pharma is only going after those who have a genetic variation of the APOL1 gene. Boehringer Ingelheim is in a phase 2 study targeting FSGS patients with its TCR6 inhibitor BI764198, and it remains to be seen if it is successful with its ongoing mid-stage trial. The point here is that there is no assurance that the data Amicus and Dimerix achieved will be good enough to fend off competition from other companies developing drugs to treat these patients with FSGS.

Conclusion

Amicus Therapeutics is in a position to continue to grow as a company. Specifically, it denotes that it expects to reach $1 billion in peak sales for both GALAFOLD and POMBILITTI and OPFOLDA. The thing is that the growth of both of these drugs is ideal enough as it is for this company, but I think it did well to generate a license agreement with Dimerix for DMX-200. The first interim analysis has already passed, showing that the drug was doing better than the placebo compared to the endpoint of proteinuria. A second blinded interim analysis is next, and this is really going to be a major inflection point to keep an eye on.

With another pass of a pre-specified interim analysis from the phase 3 ACTION3 study, it is going to be a bullish sign for the final outcome. Another milestone relating to this clinical program would be the completion of enrollment for this phase 3 study, which is expected to happen by year-end 2025. The basis for going over this program is because this is another huge market opportunity that Amicus is going to be able to go after in the United States. Plus, the fact that DMX-200 is versatile and can be applied towards the targeting of other rare renal disorders.

data.title: Tariffs Blitzkrieg
data.description:
The world has been taken by storm by the Trump Administration’s tariff strategies. At first with disbelief, then with “but that’s what caused the 1930’s depression”, and now capitulation. Trade wars are generally disastrous for global economies, even in capitulation.

Today there is a creeping disbelief in potential outcomes, perhaps it just may work, perhaps, as we have seen none of those bad economic events transpire (yet). It may just be a brilliant strategy, maybe, perhaps.

The expectation of an early impact is the only fallacy here. The economic consequences are on their way. Let’s first address the nature of tariffs and then turn to what’s on offer.

We’ll do this point-by-point, so we do not stray from the road into the gibberish swamp.

What exactly is a tariff?

  1. A tariff is a government tax on imports.
  2. Very important, it’s a tax on imports and not a tax on exports.
  3. The importer pays the tariff tax to the government, not the exporter.
  4. Tariffs are the worst kind of tax which is levied on turnover rather than profits and never bothers with the affordability question.
  5. The rule of thumb for profit after tax is 10% for businesses. Taking a 5% tariff off turnover halves that net profit margin. Taking a 10% tariff off turnover wipes that profit out. Taking a 15% tariff off turnover yields a 5% net loss. Taxing a fat profit margin on turnover may not be disruptive but taxing everything even beyond a tenable sacrifice of net profit will be very disruptive, which we will discuss below. The higher the net profit of the importer or exporter or both the better the chance would be for them to bear some tariff tax but generally businesses will not have the scope to bear 10% and 15% tariff taxes. Fat profit margins mean business leverage so the chance of them not passing the tariff tax to consumers is slim.

Who ultimately pays the tariff tax?

This is an interesting question as this is where all the debate comes from.

  1. The importer pays the tax in the first instance. There is no way around this. The importer will look to pass the cost of the tariff tax to someone else.
  2. The easiest target is the buyers and consumers of the imported product. This will be the default choice as this is the foundation principle of business. I buy something at one price and sell it to someone else at a higher price. The importer will find the best balance between sales/import volumes, costs, and profits. The importer will not “eat the tax”; no business will, or usually can, even if they like. All importers will rebalance their business activity. The importer will also look at the exporter to subsidize the tax burden.
  3. The exporter operates on the same business principles as the importer. The exporter will have a vested interest in holding market share, but in the case of a tariff tax will have no incentive to “eat the tariff” as it will just be a dead cost with no market benefit. There is no rationale for low profit trade volume or trade volume at a loss. Thus, the exporter will also just rebalance his business between sales/export volumes, costs, and profits.
  4. Promises of governments of exporters to support their exporters are empty. I have in my article “Bonds: Fiscal Discipline In Decline” discussed government finances. Governments are all massively overextended on debt and simply can’t balance their budgets. They have no scope to subsidize exporters. It’s a fantasy to expect other governments to “eat the tax.”

That is it. The consumer will pay in the first and last instance, and trade volumes will be adjusted as required to ensure that the consumer pays. The tariff tax may squeeze a few drops of blood from the supply chain, but it will not be material, the consumer will pay.

How long will it take before the consequences become visible?

This is a “how long is a piece of string?” question. The consequences will show up over time as businesses experience the tariff taxes in a feedback loop and adjust their business models. That can only happen once a tariff regime has been settled in a tariff/trade deal.

Current comment is that the tariffs have not resulted in any tangible harm “as yet” and therefore may not actually have any effect. It is far too early. Very few if any tariff dispensations have been enacted at this stage, and the feedback loops have not yet been triggered with any certainty. Let’s do these points as mileposts.

  1. Tariff and trade deals must be agreed to establish a tariff regime.
  2. Businesses under each tariff regime will adjust their business models over time to the new tariff regime.
  3. The implementation of new global tariff regimes will be a slow-moving process, and tangible consequences will only appear once; (i) enough tariff regimes have been agreed and implemented; and (ii) importers and exporters have had time to assess feedback loops and adjust their business models to the new tariff tax regime and consumer off-take.
  4. The scales will tip only after the process has gained enough momentum.

What will the consequences of tariffs be?

Small isolated tariff events would just adjust within a national or global economy, and there would only be sectoral implications. A general new global tariff taxation must express itself globally and nationally.

  1. Fact: Tariff tax takes from the population, global and national and gives to the government.
  2. Fact: Consumers will pay more for imported products.
  3. Fact: Consumption spending will remain as intended, but utility will drop as more money buys less.
  4. Fact: Imported inflation will increase as prices increase generally and the tariff tax wedge between the supplier and the consumer.
  5. Fact: Taxing consumers more results in less economic activity where consumers buy fewer products, suppliers sell fewer products, and the government takes a larger cut from the economy.
  6. Fact: Taxing imports reduces imports.
  7. Fact: Taxing imports globally reduces international trade and risks a global recession. History shows that tariffs have the potential to create depressions; it’s a high-stakes game where the negative outcomes are generally a given and a positive outcome is a unicorn. Tariffs are a known lose-lose game.

Tariff developments so far

Here is the extract from the White House communique.

Fact Sheet: President Donald J. Trump Further Modifies the Reciprocal Tariff Rates

The White House

July 31, 2025

“President Trump’s bold trade strategy has yielded historic agreements with major trading partners, unlocking unprecedented investments in the United States and expanding market access for American goods. These deals strengthen America’s economic and security positions and create opportunities for American workers, farmers, and businesses.

In a massive deal with the European Union, the EU has agreed to purchase $750 billion in U.S. energy and make new investments of $600 billion in the United States, all by 2028, while accepting a 15% tariff rate.

Japan has agreed to invest $550 billion in the United States to rebuild and expand core American industries, as well as to further open its own market to U.S. exports, all while paying a baseline 15% tariff rate.

The United States-United Kingdom trade deal includes billions of dollars of increased market access for American exports.

Additional trade deals with Indonesia, the Philippines, South Korea, Vietnam, and others will protect our industries, open foreign markets, and encourage foreign investment in American industries.”

These deals are announced with very few details and diametrically opposing interpretations by the players. Trying to analyze the current state of the “deals” is not possible not even for the businesses who would need to adapt to the changed environment. Both Japan and the EU have materially and conceptually differing views regarding the “investment” component. The tariff as well as rebalancing agreements are all murky. We can only look at these “deals” in principle.

The deals have three elements, a tariff, an investment, and exports from the USA of goods to balance trade. Trade has never been about forced balanced trade. The free-market system is based upon the free flow of goods and services which would have been balanced had we still functioned under a gold standard. Gold is a “good” and any trade imbalance would have been eliminated through a gold flow away from the trade deficit country to the trade surplus country. The trade deficit would be eliminated by the gold outflow, and the trade surplus would be eliminated by the gold inflow in an automatic rebalancing process.

It follows that endless trade deficits are impossible where gold must be relinquished to balance trade. No country could import goods unless it could settle any trade deficit through a transfer of gold. The deficit importing country would also need to curtail domestic spending and devalue its currency when its appetite for imports exceeds its ability to pay in gold. Domestic spending on imports would become more expensive due to the currency devaluation. Domestic spending would normally further be curtailed through increased interest rates and higher taxes.

The positive, over time, would be that exports would be encouraged given the lower exchange rate with the rest of the world. A pure fiat system lacks all these automatic rebalancing mechanisms, and countries whose currencies serve as international trade currencies can game the system to import and balance the deficit through money creation. Gaming the fiat money system avoids both the curtailment of spending and the currency devaluation.

Exporting countries, in turn, game the importing countries' reckless spending and saddle them with debt, a lot of which migrates to government debt via the trade surplus investment flow and as a result of the importing government not curtailing import consumption.

Another of the consequences of gaming the system with money creation without end is a relentless loss of purchasing power. The buck always stops at the consumer.

The infographic is a bit dated, but the additional information is interesting. The up-to-date chart is available from the St. Louis Fed. I’ll take two snapshots, one from 1913 when the Federal Reserve was established and one from 1971 when the world went onto a global pure fiat system.

Long-term chart of the purchasing power of the US dollar under the Federal Reserve.

The chart starts at 1000, and in June 2025 the index was at 31 giving us 31/1000, or 3.1% of the purchasing power of the USD in 1931.

USD purchasing power from August 1971 to June 2025.

It’s a one-way loss of purchasing power at 31/245.1 where the USD of today retains only 12.65% of its value of 1971.

I have already discussed the consequences of a global economy functioning in a monetary environment of pure fiat in Global Financial Markets: Slow Motion Crash Underway and how it facilitated USA trade and budget deficits for most of the last 54 years.

The “deals” announced above are accompanied by further tariff pressures globally to force more tariff “deals”. Gaming the international trade system in this manner is similar to reshaping glass with a hammer.

The attempt to rebalance trade through tariffs will fail as tariffs, as discussed above, are not a viable alternative to living within a country's means. The country must curtail its spending or devalue its currency, usually both. Tariffs can’t take the place of curtailing domestic spending on imported goods and services unless the tariff tax is passed on to the consumer as is the expected outcome of current tariffs.

The trade imbalance in US dollars is counterbalanced by investment in the USA. Countries with trade surpluses will have surplus dollars and will not hoard those dollars, they will return the dollars to the USA to invest in assets like the stock exchange and USA government debt.

Tariffs will reduce that investment flow to the extent that it impacts international trade. Let’s pause on those flows for a moment. The tariff is imposed on the US importer who will pass most of it to the US consumer which will curtail consumption of imported goods and services. The negative impact on international trade will reduce investment flow (probably the reason why the Trump Administration demands investments). Forced imports will not mitigate the negative effects, and both the "investments" and imports are unaffordable by those governments as already discussed. The tariff-imposing government will collect the tariffs from its importers, who will collect from consumers. The bottom line is unassailable; the consumers will pay.

Conclusion

I have deliberately kept this article tight and factual given the dust and noise surrounding tariffs. Expecting an outcome other than the factual framework is wishful thinking. Tariffs are a zero-sum game. What one takes, another loses. There is now a way in which one can take and nobody loses. It is a game where the government takes and consumers lose. In the USA, having the international trade currency, the impact of reduced liquidity flow from tariff curtailed international trade is a high-risk variable.

The game is played to pass the loss from one nation to another, but it never works as businesses adjust (not governments!) to the tariffs and shift the tax to consumers. No amount of threats will prevent a business from rebalancing its business model when it makes losses. Taxing turnover is always economically harmful as it is never tested against affordability. The end result is always that consumers suffer the consequences. Tariffs at this scale are not a game of Us against Them, it’s a game of the US government taxing its own consumers while playing chicken with a global recession. Tariff consequences this time will not be different.

data.title: Arcutis: Leveraging Zoryve's Success While Positioning For Pipeline Expansion
data.description:
Background

I last covered Arcutis Biotherapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:ARQT) in May 2024, rating the stock as a Hold based on the company’s reliance on Zoryve. I was skeptical about the company’s capacity to translate their two FDA approvals in plaque psoriasis and seborrheic dermatitis into significant revenue increases that would support share price surges beyond the 156% that the company had already observed between January and May 2024. But I was wrong (see image below).

Since my last article, ARQT’s shares are up by 72%. Zoryve received FDA approvals in Atopic Dermatitis, and more recently it obtained regulatory approval for a foam formulation developed for the treatment of scalp and body Plaque Psoriasis. In Q1 2025, prior to the Zoryve foam for Plaque Psoriasis launch, the company reported $63.8 million in product revenue, which represented a 196% increase when compared against the $21.6 million reported in Q1 2024. Although my concerns about Arcutis’ reliance on Zoryve remain, it is obvious that I was wrong about the company’s capacity to translate the regulatory success into commercial success.

Thesis

Arcutis Biotherapeutics is due to report Q2 2025 earnings in August 6th after market close. I don’t expect the foam formulation to have a major effect on Q2 2025 product revenue, given that it was launched in early June, but we might learn about the commercialization dynamics. More importantly, I believe quality and safety labels supporting the use of Zoryve for the treatment of Plaque Psoriasis and atopic dermatitis, as well as studies supporting the durability of its therapeutic effect on atopic dermatitis, are likely to have a positive impact on prescribers and patient awareness, which potentially contributed to bolster Q2 product revenue results.

In October, the company is expecting to receive the regulatory approval of their Zoryve label expansion on atopic dermatitis in children from 2–5 years old. In addition, ARQ-255, a treatment aimed against alopecia areata, is due to release Phase Ib results in Q3. While the IND filling associated with ARQ-234, a CD200 receptor agonist for the treatment of atopic dermatitis, is expected by H2 2025. In addition, Arcutis’ CEO declared, at the Goldman Sachs 46th Annual Global Healthcare Conference, that they are looking into business development opportunities and further Zoryve label expansions to address the company’s long-term growth. Thus, partially addressing my concerns about Arcutis’ lack of pipeline diversification.

In this article, I will be covering Arcutis’ recent pipeline updates, its relevance to investors, the financial status of the company, including my Q2 2025 expectations, and my valuation, supporting my rating upgrade from “Hold” to “Buy” for long-term investors.

Zoryve (Roflumilast) Franchise Updates

Zoryve’s active molecule is Roflumilast, whose mechanism of action inhibits the production of pro-inflammatory signals known as cytokines, in turn reducing the symptoms associated with Plaque Psoriasis, Seborrheic Dermatitis and Atopic Dermatitis. Please find more details on Roflumilast’s mechanism of action in my previous analysis.

Unlike corticosteroids, Zoryve not only provides a rapid and long-lasting therapeutic action, but it also exhibits a safe profile, enabling its use for as long and as frequent as the symptoms persist. Indeed, the National Psoriasis Foundation and the American Academy of Dermatologists are backing Zoryve’s therapeutic and safe profile for the treatment of Plaque Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis, respectively. This superior therapeutic profile has translated into Zoryve’s sustained market share growth in the topical non-steroids treatments, nearly double in Q1 2025 vs. Q1 2024, which has been critical to the 50% topical segment growth against corticosteroids in the same period of time (see image below).

Despite Zoryve’s initial slow rollout, without a doubt Arcutis’ commercial strategy and recurrent label expansions have been paying off. In this sense, the latest approved indication of Zoryve’s foam for the treatment of scalp Plaque Psoriasis is poised to boost Arcutis’ product revenue in the long term, not only providing a solution that resolves the main symptoms of this inflammatory condition but also providing patients with a formulation that doesn’t generate other issues such as greasy hair or long-term usage concerns.

Zoryve’s foam Plaque Psoriasis launch presentation showcased data demonstrating the efficacy of the formulation on significantly reducing a key inflammatory marker associated with the condition ((IgA)) after eight weeks of treatment, reaching complete scalp clearance in 40% of patients and body clearance in 27.8% (see image below).

In the case of Plaque Psoriasis, prescribers usually need to provide the patient with a list of treatments specific for each problematic area, which also involve spending a long period of time on educating their patients and warning them about the potential side effects of miss-usage or treatment usage for extended periods of time. Thus, Zoryve’s diversity of formulations and safe therapeutic profile is very attractive to both patients and prescribers, who now can provide a single prescription that can be used in all problematic areas without major safety concerns (see image below).

Overall, thanks to its capacity to treat Plaque Psoriasis, and the lack of side effects, Zoryve cream/foam has the potential to become the new standard of care against this condition, in turn having a positive impact on Arcutis product revenue performance from Q3 2025 onwards. Likewise, in October, Zoryve’s potential label expansion in Atopic Dermatitis to include children of 2–5 years old would further expand Arcutis’s product revenue projections.

ARQ-255: Alopecia Areata

ARQ-255 is Arcutis’ most advanced product candidate, after Zoryve, which aims to provide a therapeutic solution to Alopecia Areata. This autoimmune condition affects approximately 2% of the global population at least once in their lifetime, and it is characterized by smooth and circular patches of hair loss.

ARQ-255’s active molecule is Ivarmacitinib, a JAK1 inhibitor that has demonstrated efficacy in the treatment of Alopecia Areata when provided as oral treatment (i.e., pills) without causing treatment-related severe adverse events. However, ARQ-255 is aimed as a topical treatment, then requiring the treatment to penetrate deeply into the scalp’s skin to reach the affected hair follicle bulbs. To address this challenge, Arcutis is leveraging their Deep Dermal Drug Delivery proprietary technology to ensure the delivery of Ivarmacitinib.

Despite the initial slow enrollment in ARQ-255’s Phase Ib clinical trial, the company completed the participant’s enrollment in September 2025 and is expecting to share results this quarter. The Alopecia Areata’s market size is projected to grow at 7.3% CAGR from 2024 to 2034, reaching up to $6.3 billion by 2034. Although this number is not particularly impressive, to date there are no FDA-approved topical treatments indicated against Alopecia Areata, which highlights the unmet clinical need.

Similarly to Plaque Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis, Alopecia Areata is usually treated off-label with topical corticosteroids, which temporarily reduce the inflammatory signals but can only be used for short periods of time. In addition, Litfulo from Pfizer (PFE) and Olumiant from Eli Lilly (LLY) are both FDA-approved oral treatments indicated for the treatment of severe Alopecia Areata. Although PFE did not disclose the specific sales figures associated with Litfulo, Eli Lilly reported Olumiant global sales reaching $957.4 million in 2024.

Although oral JAK inhibitors have demonstrated therapeutic efficacy against Alopecia Areata, they also carry serious side effect warnings, including headaches, infections, cancer, and immune disorders, that may discourage patients from using the treatment.

In this sense, I believe investors should keep an eye on ARQ-255 upcoming Phase Ib results, which should shed some light into the capacity of this treatment to penetrate deeply into the scalp skin, as well as its safety profile. Presuming good therapeutic, safety, and regulatory outcomes, ARQ-255 could become the first and only topical treatment indicated against Alopecia Areata, bringing an additional revenue stream to Arcutis in the long term.

Financials

Arcutis Biotherapeutics reported total revenues amounting to $65.846 million in Q1 2025, representing a 44.49% increase against Q1 2024 (see image below). However, in Q1 2024, Arcutis received a $25 million upfront payment associated with Zoryve’s licensing agreement with Sato Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., while no milestone payments were received in Q1 2025. So, Arcutis’ Q1 2025 product revenue observed a 196% increase when compared to the figures reported in Q1 2024.

The company also observed an 11% increase in Q1 2025 operating expenses when compared against Q1 2024, which was attributed to higher Research & Development costs associated with the pipeline progression. Currently, the majority of the clinical trials associated with Zoryve and the ARQ-255 trial are nearing completion; thus, I do not expect the R&D expenses to exhibit a significant increase in Q2 2025. However, the company’s CFO declared in the Q1 2025 earnings call that they expect the sales and administrative expenses in Q2 2025 to be higher than Q1 2025, based on Zoryve's foam for Plaque Psoriasis launch. Given the positive momentum in total revenue, Arcutis has reported significant net loss and loss per share reductions amounting to 29% and 37%, respectively, in Q1 2025 in contrast with Q1 2024.

To date, Arcutis has observed a significant increase in total prescriptions following Zoryve’s regulatory successes (see image below). Building on that, I expect the most recent FDA approval is likely to contribute further to Zoryve’s prescription upward trend and ultimately generate more revenue. Nonetheless, given that Zoryve foam for Plaque Psoriasis was launched in June, I don’t believe this effect will be particularly strong in the upcoming Q2 report.

Overall, I estimate the Q2 revenue will continue the upward trend observed in the last year, mostly attributed to the good therapeutic results, the increasing awareness among prescribers and patients, the label expansions, and the insurance coverage. In turn, reducing further the net loss, despite not observing significant reductions in operating expenses in Q2 2025.

In terms of liquidity, the company is still observing a cash flow reduction. Nonetheless, at the Q1 2025 earnings call, Arcutis’ CFO declared the company is on track to achieve cash breakeven status in 2026, and does not expect the need for raising cash to support operations in the foreseeable future.

Valuation

ARQT’s shares are trading at $14.25, and a $1.69 billion market cap. Arcutis’ 1Y total return showed a 50% increase, outperforming the 16% increase exhibited by the S&P 500 (SP500) in the same period of time (see image below).

Zoryve’s regulatory and commercial success has supported the company’s extraordinary YoY revenue growth (100.06%), which has largely exceeded the Sector median figure, standing at 6.76%. More importantly, revenue growth forward for Arcutis Biotherapeutics projects a 93% revenue increase, while the Sector median projections stand at 8.09%. Taking this revenue growth forecast into account, the consensus expects the EPS to grow by 50% in 2025. In addition, the enterprise value is currently estimated at $1.65 billion, which stands nearly at the same level as ARQT’s market cap.

In terms of analyst sentiment, Arcutis has Buy ratings from Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts, as well as Seeking Alpha Quant rating. According to Wall Street analysts, Arcutis’ average share price target is $21.25, accounting for a 48% upside potential from today’s share price. Indeed, this share price target falls in between my intrinsic value ($17.90) and my bullish case share price ($28.7) projected in my previous analysis.

Overall, I believe ARQT offers today a solid investment thesis for long-term investors, given Zoryve’s reiterated regulatory and commercial success, supporting my rating upgrade from Hold to Buy. However, investors interested in opening or increasing positions in ARQT, should also consider the risks on such investment.

Risks

Despite Zoryve’s commercial success, the advances in the development of ARQ-255 and the upcoming IND filling associated with ARQ-234, one of the largest risks to my long-term investment thesis is Arcutis Biotherapeutics' poor pipeline. Although the lean pipeline is likely helping Arcutis to control operating costs down, they have reached a point where the majority of Zoryve’s registrational trials are completed, which then enables them to allocate some resources to accelerate the development of ARQ-255, ARQ-234 and potentially in-license other product candidates of interest. In this sense, I believe Arcutis’ management declarations, at the latest Goldman Sachs annual conference, about their interest to pursue more indications for Zoryve and business development opportunities is a good sign for investors.

Building on Arcutis’ reliance on Zoryve, another risk is coming from Roflumilast (Zoryve) attack from generics. Recently, Arcutis and Padagis agreed to pause the lawsuit associated with Zoryve’s patent infringement, resulting in the protection of Roflumilast’s exclusivity. Zoryve’s LOE is due by 2037. Although this is overall great news for Arcutis, it also highlights Arcutis’ need to fiercely protect Zoryve’s patent.

Conclusions

Arcutis is due to report Q2 2025 earnings after market close on August 6th. Based on the regulatory success, recommendations by the National Psoriasis Foundation and the American Academy of Dermatologists, durability data, and increasing positive opinion from prescribers and patients, I expect Zoryve’s prescriptions and product revenues to continue their upwards trend in Q2. Hence, supporting my rating upgrade from Hold to Buy for long-term investors.

Beyond Zoryve, Arcutis is expected to share data related with ARQ-255 Phase Ib clinical trial on patients with Alopecia Areata. Although this product candidate is still in early stages of development, I believe a positive therapeutic and safety profile could position ARQ-255 with a chance to become the first topical treatment and the best-in-class therapy against Alopecia Areata. To put things in context, despite holding a strong warning label, highlighting potential severe side effects such as infections, immune disorders, cancer, among others, Eli Lilly’s oral pill (Olumiant), reported nearly $1 billion in global sales in 2024. Thus, investors should keep an eye on ARQ-255 clinical trial results, which are due this quarter.

Overall, Arcutis’ performance over the last year has demonstrated their capacity to translate Zoryve’s regulatory success into commercial success. Looking ahead, the company needs to demonstrate their capacity to become profitable and to expand their pipeline beyond Zoryve. Based on the management declarations, the company is taking the right steps towards securing long-term stability and profitability. Nonetheless, investors interested in the stock should also be aware of the risks listed in this article and elsewhere, and implement the necessary risk mitigating strategies.

data.title: Boot Barn: Executing Well, But Priced For Perfection
data.description:
One of the names we have traded in the specialty retail space for gains is Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BOOT). Shares have roared higher off the lows from April 2025, and are getting very expensive valuation-wise. With 10% plus short interest, a seasonally weak period for the market, and this valuation reality, we are on the sidelines. Traders should strongly consider taking some profit here, and new money, in our opinion, really needs to wait for a pullback at these levels. Earnings will only grow about 8% this fiscal year based on consensus, which is very low growth for this pricing. It is pricing in ongoing big beats against the consensus, and we just think the risk-reward is imbalanced at this point. The company just reported earnings, and shares are modestly higher, despite a pretty decent beat on the top- and bottom-line. In this column, we discuss the company and the just-reported fiscal Q1 earnings and why we think a neutral rating makes sense here.

Who is Boot Barn?

This company has been operating for 4 decades. For those unfamiliar, Boot Barn is the largest and is the most rapidly growing western bootwear retailer in the United States. On the bullish front, they are in expansion mode and still have a big runway for growth. They now have 475 stores in 49 states after opening over 75 new stores in the last year. The company, of course, also sells many products online in addition to its brick-and-mortar locations. Boot Barn offers a wide variety of cowboy boots, work boots, western wear, workwear, and outdoor gear and offers services, like in-person boot fitting services, cleaning services, and hat shaping. The company is set to expand more this fiscal year, targeting another 65-70 stores to be opened.

Boot Barn Fiscal Q1 revenues and comparable sales

With the expansion of new stores, we saw sales expand from last year, and these sales surpassed consensus estimates by $8.9 million. Net sales were up 19.1% compared to the comparable year-ago period to $504 million.. In retail, the number one metric we watch is same-store (or comparable-store) sales. Same-store sales increased 9.4% compared to the prior-year period. The 9.4% increase in same-store sales was due to retail same-store sales rising 9.5% in brick and mortar stores, and a 9.3% increase in online sales. So, while being priced for growth, on sales, said growth is being delivered, even in an uncertain economic time.

Margins expanding

So, despite the short interest, shares are holding up thanks to strong comparable sales, but also the earnings power on those sales has improved. We saw margin expansion. Gross profit overall was $197.2 million, or 39.1% of net sales, compared to $156.7 million, or 37.0% of net sales a year ago. So this margin and gross profit expansion was a result of higher merchandise margins. There was a 180 basis point improvement in merchandise margin, reflecting lower promotion and higher tickets, and we saw 30 basis points of improvement thanks to leverage in occupancy and distribution costs. Now, there is tariff exposure. And to offset this, the company has been re-ticketing impacted items and expects to maintain its merchandise margin rate, as noted in the conference call.

Boot Barn earnings power

So we saw increasing comparable sales, better than expected top-line revenue, and widening gross margins. This is a recipe for success, even at this valuation. Factoring in selling and operating expenses, we saw Boot Barn’s income from operations jump dramatically by $20.5 million to $70.7 million, or 14.0% of net sales, compared to $50.2 million, or 11.9% of net sales a year ago. As such, we saw an earnings beat. Net income was $53.4 million, or $1.74 per share, compared to net income of $38.9 million, or $1.26 per share a year ago, besting estimates by $0.20.

Forward view on Boot Barn’s fiscal 2026

Here is the deal. This was a solid quarter, no doubt. But at this valuation, shares are priced for ongoing strong growth. And despite a positive outlook, the projections for this year do not justify today's price. Of course, shares of any stock can remain at a premium for a long time. But we think it is an uphill battle against the short interest and the fact that each quarter needs to be a beat (and really a raised outlook) to keep shares so elevated. On the positive side, we like the continued geographic expansion. For the year, the company has guided EPS of $5.80 to $6.70, or $6.25 at the midpoint. Folks, at the time of this writing, shares are trading at $179.50. That is a 28.7X FWD EPS estimate. If the midpoint is hit, that is growth of just 7% from last year. That is a pricing mismatch at these levels. As such, one weak quarter and shares will get hard, in our opinion. Not to mention, the market is coming into a weak period. At these levels, consider taking profit and look to come back in on a correction. New money should wait, in our opinion based, on this growth.
